overview.body: ENHANCE week. Two big wins land together. First, hazards move out of lab-4-2.py and into hazards.txt, loaded into a list of dictionaries with a third loader function. Second, .upper() finally arrives. A new clean_input() helper strips whitespace and uppercases the result, collapsing every y/Y/n/N comparison into a single Y/N check. get_valid_int also gets smarter, using .isdigit() so typing abc at a numeric prompt no longer crashes the program. Two Patch Notes Tyler dropped back in Module 2 finally close this week.

mentorQuote.quote: The player should never lose to bad input. They typed y instead of Y, the game crashed, they reload, they're annoyed. That's a design failure, not a player failure. clean_input() means we accept whatever the player types, uppercase, lowercase, with a stray space, and meet them where they are. That's what good interfaces feel like: invisible.

objectives: Rewrite get_valid_int in the library to use .isdigit() so non-numeric input is caught with a friendly error message instead of crashing with ValueError (closes Tyler's Patch Note from Lab 2-3, CP3)
Rewrite get_yes_no in the library to use clean_input. The 4-way 'y'/'Y'/'n'/'N' comparison collapses to a single Y/N check
Add load_hazards(filename) to the library, parsing hazards.txt into a list of dictionaries (closes Tyler's Patch Note from Lab 2-2, CP6)
Add handle_hazard(hazards) to the library: picks a random hazard, prints the encounter, flips the escape coin, returns a tuple (name, escaped)

In lab-4-2.py: copy 4.1 forward, delete the inline hazard parallel lists, load hazards.txt at startup, replace the inline hazard block in the travel loop with a single handle_hazard call, and retire get_random_hazard entirely
overview.keyConceptsText: .upper() and .strip() compose elegantly. input(prompt).strip().upper() reads from the user, removes any surrounding whitespace, and converts the result to uppercase, all in one expression. Wrapping that pattern in clean_input() means every input cleanup in the game looks the same. .isdigit() checks whether a string consists entirely of decimal digits, which is the right test for 'is this a non-negative whole number?' before calling int(). List of dictionaries is the third dictionary shape you've seen this module: a list whose items happen to be dicts. random.choice(hazards) returns one of those dicts, and hazard['name'] retrieves a field.
overview.textbookReference: Chapter 8: Programs 8–1 through 8–14 cover string methods. .upper() and .isdigit() are demonstrated in Programs 8–6 and 8–7. Chapter 9 Program 9–10 shows a list of dictionaries.
programRequirements: Top-of-file comment block on both files: Name, abc123, Section, Description
Load all three data files at startup: city_distances.txt, city_info.txt, hazards.txt
Three-loop architecture preserved from 4.1: main menu, play-again, in-game
Hazards loaded from hazards.txt; no inline hazard parallel lists in lab-4-2.py
Trip report still saved to trip_report.txt at the end of every completed game

overview.filenameNote: Submit lab-4-2.py and function_library.py. CodeGrade provides all three data files as fixtures.

dataFiles.format: Shared function library, extended this week with clean_input, load_hazards, and handle_hazard

rows.task: Copy lab-4-1.py forward and add load_hazards to the library
Add clean_input to the library: strip + upper
Rewrite get_valid_int with .isdigit() so non-numeric input no longer crashes
Rewrite get_yes_no on top of clean_input
Promote hazard handling to a library function returning (name, escaped)
Delete inline hazard lists from lab-4-2.py, load hazards.txt, swap in handle_hazard
Verify the trip report still shows hazards correctly with the new dict-shape data
Full play-through, then upload both files to CodeGrade

beforeYouBegin.intro: Lab 4.2 is mostly library work. The lab file changes in two places: the startup loads one more file, and the in-game hazard block collapses to a single function call. Everything else carries forward from 4.1. [NEW] tags show what's added this week. [REPLACED] tags show what swaps out. [RETIRED] tags show what gets deleted. [KEEP] tags show what carries forward.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- IN function_library.py ----
flip_coin(), roll_d20(), check_percentage()                 [KEEP from 2.2]
save_game(...), load_game()                                 [KEEP from 3.2]
display_destinations(city, distances)                       [KEEP from 4.1]
get_destination(city, distances, choice)                    [KEEP from 4.1]
get_redirect(city, distances, avoid)                        [KEEP from 4.1]
is_win_city(city, city_info)                                [KEEP from 4.1]
is_dead_end(city, city_info)                                [KEEP from 4.1]
load_city_distances(filename), load_city_info(filename)     [KEEP from 4.1]
display_numbered_list(items, title)                         [KEEP from 3.2]

clean_input(prompt) -> strip + upper                        [NEW-CP2]
get_valid_int(prompt, min_val, max_val)                     [REPLACED-CP3] now uses .isdigit()
get_yes_no(prompt)                                          [REPLACED-CP4] now uses clean_input
load_hazards(filename) -> list of dicts                     [NEW-CP1]
handle_hazard(hazards) -> (name, escaped)                   [NEW-CP5]
get_random_hazard(...)                                      [RETIRED-CP5]

# ---- IN lab-4-2.py ----

# ---- CONSTANTS ----
DISTANCES_FILE, INFO_FILE, REPORT_FILE                      [KEEP from 4.1]
HAZARDS_FILE = 'hazards.txt'                                [NEW-CP6]
MILES_MULTIPLIER = 10, HAZARD_CHANCE = 30                   [KEEP from 4.1]

HAZARD_NAMES, HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS, HAZARD_TAILS_MSGS          [RETIRED-CP6] now in hazards.txt

save_trip_report(filename, ...)                             [KEEP from 4.1]

# ---- main() ----
distances = load_city_distances(...)                        [KEEP from 4.1]
city_info = load_city_info(...)                             [KEEP from 4.1]
hazards   = load_hazards(HAZARDS_FILE)                      [NEW-CP6]

MAIN MENU LOOP                                              [KEEP from 4.1]
  PLAY-AGAIN LOOP                                           [KEEP from 4.1]
    IN-GAME LOOP                                            [KEEP from 4.1]
      │
      │─ D20 travel loop
      │     │─ if hazard triggers:
      │     │     name, escaped = handle_hazard(hazards)   [REPLACED-CP6]
      │     │     hazards_list.append(name)
      │     │     if escaped: hazards_escaped += 1
      │
      │─ Continue / Save & quit / Quit                     [KEEP from 4.1]
    
    Trip report on screen + trip_report.txt                 [KEEP from 4.1]
    Session accumulators                                    [KEEP from 4.1]

content.body: A clean starting point for Lab 4.2. Copy lab-4-1.py to lab-4-2.py, update the docstring, and add one new function to function_library.py: load_hazards(filename). It reads hazards.txt using the exact same .strip().split(',') pattern from Lab 4.1, but returns a list of dictionaries instead of a nested dict.

This is the moment Tyler called back in Lab 2.2, CP6, when he warned that the hardcoded hazard data would not scale. He was right. Lab 4.1 still defined hazards as three parallel lists at the top of the lab file. This week those three lists go away and the same data lives in hazards.txt, parsed once at startup. The Patch Note closes here.

content.quote: Notice the loader pattern is the same one you wrote twice already. .strip().split(',') per line, one dictionary or list per row. The shape of what you build differs by purpose, but the parsing is identical. That is the sign the design is right. The boring part is reusable.

items: Copy lab-4-1.py to lab-4-2.py in the same folder.
Update the docstring at the top of lab-4-2.py: change the title to Lab 4.2: Flexible Input and update the description.
Do not copy function_library.py. It stays in place; you extend it directly.
In function_library.py, add a new section header comment block titled HAZARD FUNCTIONS (Added in Lab 4-2). Place it after the city data loaders.
Define load_hazards(filename) with a docstring describing the list-of-dicts return shape.
Inside the function, create an empty list called hazards.
Open the file with infile = open(filename, 'r'). Loop with for line in infile:.
For each line, call parts = line.strip().split(','). Build a dictionary hazard = {'name': parts[0], 'heads': parts[1], 'tails': parts[2]} and hazards.append(hazard).
Close the file and return hazards.
Run lab-4-2.py as-is. Confirm it still plays exactly like 4.1 did.

references.description: Same .split(',') per line as Lab 4.1, but you build a different shape on the way out.
List of dictionaries is the third dictionary shape this module. Each list item is its own dictionary with the same set of keys.

content.body: Add a temporary DEBUG block under the if __name__ == '__main__': guard in function_library.py. Run the library directly to confirm the hazards load. Delete the block before moving on.

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 2
hazards = load_hazards('hazards.txt')
print(f'Loaded {len(hazards)} hazards.')
print('First hazard:', hazards[0])
print('First hazard name:', hazards[0]['name'])
print('Last hazard tails:', hazards[-1]['tails'])

content.output:
Loaded 8 hazards.
First hazard: {'name': "Buc-ee's appears!", 'heads': "You resist the beaver's call", 'tails': 'Lost 2 hours on snacks'}
First hazard name: Buc-ee's appears!
Last hazard tails: GPS is disappointed

rows: Returning a single dictionary instead of a list of dictionaries
Each line in the file is its own hazard. The function appends each one to the hazards list. The return type is list, not dict.
Mixing up parts[1] and parts[2]
Column order in hazards.txt is name,heads_message,tails_message. heads goes with parts[1], tails with parts[2].
Editing lab-4-1.py directly instead of copying first
Always preserve last week's submission. Copy first, then edit the copy.

items: lab-4-2.py exists, plays identically to lab-4-1.py at this point
function_library.py has a new HAZARD FUNCTIONS section with load_hazards(filename) defined inside
DEBUG output confirms 8 hazards load with the expected first-name and last-tails strings
DEBUG block is removed before moving on

content.body: One small function in function_library.py that you have been waiting for since Module 1: clean_input(prompt). It calls input, strips surrounding whitespace, uppercases the result, and returns it. One line of code; semester-changing in its impact.

Every time you wrote if choice == 'y' or choice == 'Y': in Modules 1, 2, and 3, you were working around the fact that .upper() had not arrived yet. It has now. From this checkpoint forward, every input-cleanup looks the same: answer = clean_input(prompt), then compare to a single uppercase string.

content.quote: Three lines of code from a textbook can change how an entire game feels. Strip, upper, return. Players will never see this function. They will only see that the game stopped fighting them about whether to press the caps-lock key. That is the definition of a good interface: the player does not notice it.

items: In function_library.py, add a new section header comment block titled STRING UTILITIES (Added in Lab 4-2). Place it just before the VALIDATION FUNCTIONS section so that the validation helpers can call clean_input later in CP3 and CP4.
Define clean_input(prompt) with a one-line body: return input(prompt).strip().upper().
Add a docstring describing the strip + uppercase behavior and what it returns.
Do not modify any other functions yet. CP3 and CP4 are where the existing helpers get rewritten on top of clean_input.

references.description: Demonstrates .upper() and .lower(). Same idea here, but chained with .strip() on the same line.

content.body: Quick interactive test: run python3 function_library.py and add a DEBUG block that calls clean_input. Type input with mixed case and leading/trailing whitespace; confirm the result comes back uppercase and trimmed. Delete the DEBUG block before moving on.

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 3
result = clean_input('Say something: ')
print(f'You typed (cleaned): {result!r}')

rows: Forgetting the return keyword
Without return, the function does the work but hands back None. The caller has nothing to compare against.
Calling .strip() after .upper()
Order does not matter here in practice (both pure operations), but the canonical order is .strip().upper(): strip first, then transform.
Returning .upper() on already-uppercased data
No-op cost, no correctness cost. Do not stress about double-uppering somewhere downstream.

items: function_library.py has a new STRING UTILITIES section with clean_input(prompt) defined inside
Running the library directly with a DEBUG call confirms the function strips whitespace and uppercases the result
Docstring present

content.body: A rewrite of get_valid_int in function_library.py. The old version called int(input(prompt)) and crashed with ValueError the moment a player typed something non-numeric. The new version uses .isdigit() to check the input first and reprompts with a friendly error message if the input is not a whole number.

Tyler flagged this gap back in Lab 2-3, CP3, when he noted that the original get_valid_int handled out-of-range numbers but not non-numeric input. That gap has been quietly waiting through Modules 2 and 3 because Chapter 8 had not happened yet. Chapter 8 happens now. The Patch Note closes.

content.quote: I have been waiting on this one. Back in 2-3 we agreed the function caught out-of-range numbers but would faceplant on abc or an empty string. The fix is .isdigit() from Chapter 8. Two lines of logic and the function stops crashing. The pattern works for any 'is this a whole number?' validation you ever write.

items: In function_library.py, find get_valid_int. The whole body gets replaced.
New body: declare valid = False and value = 0 as initial state.
Open a while not valid: loop.
Inside the loop, get user_input = input(prompt).strip(). Strip handles trailing whitespace.
Check if user_input.isdigit():. If yes, cast to int and check the range. Set valid = True if in range, otherwise print a friendly out-of-range message.

Add an elif branch for negative numbers: elif user_input.startswith('-') and user_input[1:].isdigit(): casts and range-checks the same way. The road trip game never uses negatives, but the library function should handle them in case future labs do.
Add an else branch that prints 'ERROR: Please enter a valid whole number.' with no value-set. The loop reprompts.
Outside the loop, return value.
Update the docstring to mention that the function now validates non-numeric input gracefully.

references.description: Demonstrates .isdigit(), .isalpha(), and friends. .isdigit() is the right tool for 'is this a non-negative whole number?'

content.body: Run lab-4-2.py and test each kind of bad input at any numeric menu: type abc, 3.5, an empty Enter, and 99 (out of range). All four should produce a friendly error and reprompt. Then enter a valid choice and confirm the game continues normally.

rows: Forgetting .strip() on the input
'3 '.isdigit() is False because of the trailing space. .strip() first.
Casting before checking .isdigit()
If you cast unconditionally, you reintroduce the very crash you are trying to prevent.
Returning the wrong scope of value
value is declared OUTSIDE the loop. If you declare it inside, you cannot return it after the loop exits.
Forgetting to reset valid if the range check fails
valid only flips to True when both the type check and the range check pass. If you accidentally set it to True before checking the range, the loop exits with a bad value.

items: get_valid_int rewritten with .isdigit() as the first check
Typing abc at a numeric menu produces 'ERROR: Please enter a valid whole number.' and reprompts: no crash, no traceback
Out-of-range numeric input still produces the existing out-of-range message
Updated docstring noting the Patch Note closure

content.body: A rewrite of get_yes_no on top of clean_input. The Lab 3.x version compared the raw input against four possibilities: 'y', 'Y', 'n', 'N'. The new version calls clean_input, which uppercases the result, and compares against just 'Y' and 'N'. Same behavior, half the comparison.

This is the visible relief moment for players. Before this CP, a stray caps-lock would crash the yes/no flow. After this CP, the player can type any of y, Y, y , Y\t and get the same result. The fix is half a line in get_yes_no and one new library function from CP2.

content.quote: Read the old get_yes_no body and the new one back-to-back. The old one looked like it was bracing for combat: four comparisons just to figure out whether the player meant yes. The new one just asks the user, normalizes the answer, and checks two values. The shorter version is also the friendlier one. Most of the time, that correlation holds.

items: In function_library.py, find get_yes_no. The body gets rewritten.
Replace the first answer = input(prompt) with answer = clean_input(prompt).
Replace the 4-way comparison loop condition with while answer != 'Y' and answer != 'N':.
Replace the loop-body re-read with answer = clean_input(prompt).
Replace the final return with return answer == 'Y': one comparison instead of two.
Update the docstring to mention that the rewrite uses clean_input.

references.description: Same .upper() normalization, just wrapped in clean_input for reuse. get_yes_no becomes a thin wrapper.

content.body: Run lab-4-2.py. At any yes/no prompt (Play again, Return to San Antonio), try each of: y, Y, n, N, and also y (with surrounding spaces). All five should behave correctly. Try maybe to confirm the error path still fires.

rows: Keeping the old 4-way OR condition
If the loop still says answer != 'y' and answer != 'Y' and ..., the rewrite did not land. clean_input already uppercased the answer; uppercase compare is enough.
Comparing against lowercase
clean_input returns uppercase. Compare to 'Y' and 'N', not 'y' and 'n'.
Accepting YES as yes
The function still only accepts a single-character answer. Strings like YES or YEAH are rejected. That is intentional: the contract is unchanged from Lab 3.x; only the case-handling improved.

items: get_yes_no rewritten to use clean_input
Loop condition collapsed from a 4-way OR to a 2-way comparison
Mixed-case y/n input behaves identically
YES is still rejected (single-character answers only)

content.body: handle_hazard(hazards) in function_library.py: a single function that picks a random hazard from the loaded list, prints the encounter banner, flips the escape coin, prints the matching outcome message, and returns a tuple (hazard_name, escaped). This consolidates what used to be 14 lines of inline code in the lab file.

Module 3 had get_random_hazard in the library, but it was a picker only: it returned three values for the lab file to print and flip on. With hazards now stored as dicts, the lab no longer needs to know the internal shape. handle_hazard takes the whole orchestration into the library and returns just what the lab needs: the hazard name to record and whether the player escaped. get_random_hazard retires this CP.

content.quote: When you move logic from the lab file into the library, ask what the lab actually needs back. Module 3's picker returned three strings because the lab still had to do the printing and the coin flip. Module 4's handler returns two values because the lab only needs to know what to record and whether to bump the escape counter. Smaller return shape, less knowledge required at the call site. That is a refactor.

items: In function_library.py, in the HAZARD FUNCTIONS section you started in CP1, add a second function: handle_hazard(hazards) with a full docstring.
Inside the function, pick a random hazard with hazard = random.choice(hazards).
Print '*** HAZARD! ***' and then hazard['name'] on the next line.
Flip the escape coin with flip = flip_coin(). Print the result.
If flip == 'HEADS': print hazard['heads'] and return (hazard['name'], True).
Otherwise print hazard['tails'] and return (hazard['name'], False).
Delete the old get_random_hazard function from the library entirely. Do not leave it commented out.

references.description: random.choice(hazards) returns one dictionary from the list. hazard['name'] retrieves a field. Same pattern you used at the BookEx.

content.body: Add a DEBUG block in function_library.py that loads hazards and exercises handle_hazard five times. Confirm you see five different hazard banners and a mix of HEADS / TAILS outcomes. Delete the block before moving on.

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 6
hazards = load_hazards('hazards.txt')
for i in range(5):
    name, escaped = handle_hazard(hazards)
    print(f'  -> returned: {name!r}, escaped={escaped}')
    print()

rows: Returning the whole hazard dictionary
The lab file only needs the name to append to hazards_list. Return just hazard['name'], not the dict.
Forgetting to print the outcome message
handle_hazard does both the heads/tails branching AND the message-print. The lab file does not handle messages anymore.
Leaving get_random_hazard in the library
It needs to be deleted, not just unused. CodeGrade's scope enforcer flags retired functions.

items: handle_hazard defined in the HAZARD FUNCTIONS section
Five DEBUG runs produce five different hazards (with high probability) and a mix of HEADS/TAILS
get_random_hazard is deleted from the library
Docstring describes the return tuple

content.body: The lab-file integration step. Delete the three inline hazard parallel lists from lab-4-2.py. Add HAZARDS_FILE = 'hazards.txt' to the constants block. Add a third file-load call to main() at startup. Replace the entire inline hazard block in the in-game travel loop with a single handle_hazard call. Update the hazard count display in the load banner.

This is where the library work from CPs 1–5 lands in the game. The shortening of the lab file is dramatic: about 35 lines of hazard data and orchestration collapse to a single function call. The total program does the same thing it always did, with less of it living in the lab file.

items: In lab-4-2.py, add HAZARDS_FILE = 'hazards.txt' to the constants block alongside DISTANCES_FILE and INFO_FILE.
Delete the three module-level lists HAZARD_NAMES, HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS, HAZARD_TAILS_MSGS entirely. The accompanying comment block goes with them.
In main()'s data-loading block, add hazards = function_library.load_hazards(HAZARDS_FILE) after the two existing load calls. Add a print line: print(f'Loaded {len(hazards)} possible hazards.').

Find the inline hazard block in the in-game loop. It currently has the if check_percentage(HAZARD_CHANCE): guard followed by 12 lines of orchestration. Replace those 12 lines with: name, escaped = function_library.handle_hazard(hazards), then hazards_list.append(name), then if escaped: hazards_escaped = hazards_escaped + 1.

Run the program and play a full game. Confirm hazards still fire at roughly 30% per turn, that the trip report's HAZARDS section shows the hazard names correctly, and that the escape count makes sense relative to the faced count.

references.description: The lab file does not need to know that hazards are a list of dicts anymore. handle_hazard hides that detail. The lab file just unpacks the returned tuple.

content.body: Run a full game. Test each path: beach win, dead-end at Laredo with return-no, and save-and-quit at Austin followed by a load. Confirm hazards fire, the trip report shows them by name (not as dict literals), and the saved file still works.

rows: Forgetting to unpack the tuple
Writing function_library.handle_hazard(hazards) without name, escaped = on the left throws the return value away. The hazard prints work, but hazards_list and the escape counter never update.
Appending the whole tuple to hazards_list
hazards_list.append((name, escaped)) stores 2-tuples; the trip report then displays them as ('Buc-ee\'s appears!', True). Append just name.
Putting hazards = load_hazards(...) inside the main-menu loop
Load once, before the loop. Loading inside the loop re-reads the file every time the player returns to the main menu.
Leaving the old hazard parallel-list comment block in
Delete the lists AND the explanatory comment. The whole block is gone this week.

items: HAZARDS_FILE constant declared alongside the other file constants
All three inline hazard lists deleted from lab-4-2.py
main() loads hazards.txt at startup and reports the count
In-game hazard block is one call plus three follow-up lines (append, escape check, counter)
Trip report's HAZARDS section shows clean hazard names, not dict literals

content.body: Final verification step. save_trip_report in lab-4-2.py should not need any changes: you fixed the hazard plumbing in CP6 by appending just the name string to hazards_list, which is what the trip report has always wanted. This checkpoint runs end-to-end and confirms that the on-screen report and trip_report.txt both produce the right shape with the new data flow.

Module 3 ended with a polished trip report file. Module 4.1 carried that report forward unchanged. The risk this week is that the hazard data-shape switch broke the report in a subtle way (dict literals showing up instead of names, sort order scrambled, and so on). T-Bone's QA pass here catches anything CP6 missed.

content.quote: Look at the trip report after a game with several hazards. Each line under HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED should look like 1. Buc-ee's appears!, not 1. {'name': "Buc-ee's appears!", 'heads': ..., 'tails': ...}. If the dict literal version shows up, it means CP6 appended the dict instead of just the name. Go fix that one line before submitting.

items: Run the program. Play a complete game to the beach via Corpus Christi (choice 1 from San Antonio). Let the D20 mechanic carry you forward; you should hit a few hazards along the way.
Read the on-screen trip report. Verify the HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED section shows hazards by name only (e.g., 1. Buc-ee's appears!), alphabetically sorted.
Open trip_report.txt from the same folder. Verify the same shape and ordering. The file output should match the on-screen output line-for-line.
Play a quick second game that ends in a dead end. Confirm the trip report still writes correctly with the 'Dead End' status.
Save-and-quit at a non-beach city. Confirm trip_report.txt is NOT overwritten on save-and-quit (the file from the previous game should still be there).

references.description: .copy().sort() on the hazard names list, same pattern as Lab 3.3. The names are strings (because CP6 appended just the name), so sorting works correctly.

content.body: Confirm both outputs (screen + file) are clean. Mid-game inputs you can test: y, Y, y , n, N all behave the same; abc at a numeric prompt no longer crashes.

rows: Trip report shows dict literals like {'name': 'Buc-ee\'s appears!', ...}
CP6 appended the wrong thing. Find the hazards_list.append(...) call and confirm it appends name, not the full hazard tuple or dict.
Sort order looks wrong

Hazards are sorted alphabetically by name. Apostrophes and exclamation points sort before letters in ASCII, so !-laden names like Buc-ee's appears! may sort earlier than expected. That is correct Python behavior, not a bug.
trip_report.txt contains last week's content
Make sure CP6 did not accidentally break the save_trip_report call. If the file write is not happening, the file will hold whatever was there from a previous lab.

items: On-screen trip report shows hazards by name, alphabetically
trip_report.txt matches the on-screen version line-for-line
Dead-end status writes the correct status string
Save-and-quit does not overwrite the trip report from a prior completed game

beforeYouSubmit: lab-4-2.py defines six module-level constants including the new HAZARDS_FILE
The three inline hazard parallel lists from Lab 4.1 are deleted from lab-4-2.py, gone, not commented out
main() loads three data files at startup; the load banner reports 10 cities, 10 info records, and 8 hazards
Hazard block in the in-game loop is one call: name, escaped = function_library.handle_hazard(hazards)
Mixed-case y/n input works at every yes-or-no prompt: y and Y both behave the same way, as do n and N
Typing abc at a numeric prompt no longer crashes the program
Top-of-file comment block present on both files; docstrings on every function
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Module 4.2 introduces .upper() and .isdigit() for input handling, plus a list-of-dictionaries data shape for hazards. Sets and the trivia gate are held back for Lab 4.3.

permittedTechniques.chapters: Chapters 1–9, with full Chapter 8 string methods now in scope
permittedTechniques.specific: Chapter 8: .strip(), .split(','), .upper(), .lower(), .isdigit(). Chapter 9: dictionary creation, access, nesting, list of dictionaries, random.choice() on a list of dicts. Everything from Modules 1–3 and Lab 4.1 carries forward.
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: Sets (saved for Lab 4.3). Trivia gate (saved for Lab 4.3). Dictionary comprehensions (not covered this semester). get_random_hazard is retired and should not appear in submitted code.

warnings: Do not keep the old HAZARD_NAMES / HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS / HAZARD_TAILS_MSGS lists in lab-4-2.py, even commented out. They are deleted entirely this week.
Do not leave the old call function_library.get_random_hazard(...) anywhere in either file.
finalChecklist.saveYourCode: Save lab-4-2.py as lab-4-2-backup.py before starting Lab 4.3. Lab 4.3 starts by copying 4.2 forward.

ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP6 (Wire It All Together). The data file must be in the same folder as lab-4-2.py. CodeGrade provides it as a fixture.
Check CP6. The hazard block in the in-game loop still calls the old function. Replace it with the new handle_hazard call.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: ValueError: invalid literal for int() at a menu prompt
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP3 (Smarter get_valid_int). The .isdigit() rewrite did not land in the library, or the wrong version of function_library.py is being used.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Yes/no prompt accepts y but rejects Y (or vice versa)
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP4 (Smarter get_yes_no). The rewrite uses clean_input, which uppercases its input, so the comparison should be against 'Y' and 'N' only.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Trip report's HAZARDS section shows dict-shaped strings like {'name': 'Buc-ee\'s appears!', ...}
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP6. The code is appending the entire hazard dictionary instead of just the name field. handle_hazard returns a tuple (name, escaped); unpack it and append name only.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Hazard prints fire but the trip report shows 0 hazards faced
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP6. The counter increment moved into the wrong block, or the escaped return value is being checked for the wrong outcome.

ifYouGetStuck: Re-read Programs 8–6 (.upper()) and 8–7 (.isdigit()) before you start CP2 and CP3. These two BookEx programs are exactly the pattern you need

Test the library functions in isolation before wiring them into the lab. Run python3 function_library.py after each library checkpoint. If clean_input or handle_hazard is broken, you want to know that before you start hooking them into 200 lines of game code
If your hazards never fire, check that HAZARDS_FILE is in the same folder as lab-4-2.py and the file has 8 lines
If your in-game loop crashes on the first hazard with a TypeError, double-check the unpacking: name, escaped = function_library.handle_hazard(hazards). Forgetting the unpacking is a common slip
Post in #labs on Discord with your specific error message. Include the line number, not a screenshot of the whole file
needHelp.officeHours: Zoom Tuesdays 6 PM: https://utsa.zoom.us/j/97617245124. Book a 1-on-1 with Prof. Newsom: calendly.com/john-newsom-utsa/student-meeting.
